children.value: Why Sensor Noise Is Gaussian, and How to Budget It

children.label: Why Sensor Noise Is Gaussian, and How to Budget It

children.value: Chapter 2 asserted that photon arrivals are a Poisson process and that pixel noise is approximately Gaussian. This chapter derives both claims from first principles, building the distribution chain: Bernoulli → Binomial → Poisson → Normal → CLT.

is a function that maps an experiment’s outcome to a number. Every pixel value is a random variable — it takes a different value each time you photograph the same scene, because photon arrivals are random.
completely describes a random variable: it assigns a probability to every possible value.
The three quantities that characterise a distribution:

σ2=E[(X−μ)2]\sigma^2 = E[(X-\mu)^2]σ2=E[(X−μ)2]

children.value: Central Limit Theorem — why sums are always Gaussian

children.label: Central Limit Theorem — why sums are always Gaussian

children.value: The CLT says: the sum of any

children.html: nnn

children.value: independent, identically distributed random variables with finite mean and variance converges to a Gaussian as

children.html: n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞

children.value: In a sensor, multiple noise sources add together (shot noise + read noise + dark current). Even if each source is non-Gaussian, their sum is approximately Gaussian. The CLT is why we can model total sensor noise as Gaussian in almost all practical cases.

children.html: σshot=λ\sigma_{shot} = \sqrt{\lambda}σshot​=λ​

children.value: This cannot be reduced by better hardware — it is a fundamental property of the discrete nature of light. To halve the shot noise you must quadruple the photon count (larger aperture, longer exposure, bigger photosite).

children.html: SNR=λλ=λ\text{SNR} = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \sqrt{\lambda}SNR=λ​λ​=λ​

children.value: Doubling the photon count improves SNR by

children.html: 2≈41%\sqrt{2} \approx 41\%2​≈41%

children.value: A real sensor has multiple noise sources. The total noise variance is their sum (assuming independence):

children.value: In bright conditions, shooting more light always helps. In dim conditions, read noise dominates and more photons don’t help much until you are out of the read-noise floor.
Poisson noise has variance equal to its mean — which makes it hard to work with statistically (the noise level depends on the signal level). The
converts Poisson noise to approximately unit-variance Gaussian:
